# **Analysis of Forecast Accuracy and Verification Statistics for Miami International Airport and WFO Miami Temperature Predictions**

## **Executive Summary**

The National Weather Service (NWS) Weather Forecast Office (WFO) in Miami (MFL) demonstrates its highest skill within the 0–48 hour short-range window. The implementation of the National Blend of Models (NBM) v5.0 in April 2026 has significantly enhanced this capability, extending high-resolution hourly guidance from the previous 36-hour limit to a full 48-hour window. Temperature forecasts for Miami International Airport (KMIA) are characterized by exceptional stability compared to continental U.S. stations, with a 24-hour Mean Absolute Error (MAE) typically residing between ![][image1] and ![][image2].  
The "felt" heat index remains a critical verification challenge; while KMIA station readings are highly accurate, hyperlocal data from urban neighborhoods often record temperatures ![][image3] higher due to the urban heat island (UHI) effect.1 For prediction market participants, the 48-hour window represents the primary arena for capturing informational edges, particularly regarding the timing of sea breeze fronts and convective cooling, which frequently cause transient temperature plateaus.

## **Hour-by-Hour Quantitative Verification Metrics (0–48 Hours)**

As of 2026, the NWS Meteorological Development Laboratory (MDL) verifies KMIA temperature forecasts using the Analysis of Record (URMA) as the ground truth. The table below provides the estimated hourly degradation of skill and the probability of the actual temperature falling within specific error buckets (![][image4] to ![][image5]).

### **Hourly MAE and Error Distribution Probabilities for KMIA**

| Lead Time | MAE (∘F) | Within ±1∘F | Within ±2∘F | Within ±3∘F | Within ±4∘F | Within ±5∘F | Within ±6∘F |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **1h** | **![][image6]** | **![][image7]** | **![][image8]** | **![][image9]** | **![][image10]** | **![][image11]** | **![][image12]** |

*Note: Probabilities are cumulative. "Within ![][image231]" means the absolute error is ![][image232]. Statistics are modeled using URMA-based calibration archives for WFO Miami.*

## **Historical Trends and Foundational Studies of Miami Forecast Skill**

### **The Strassberg Grid-Based Verification Project (2009)**

Gordon Strassberg's research at WFO Miami shifted verification from point-based METAR checks to 5km grid-to-grid comparisons.2 A key finding for the short-range window was the **Cold Front Overshoot**: human forecasters often outperform models two days prior to a front, but on the day of the event (D-1), they tend to overcompensate for model warmth, trending ![][image233] to ![][image3] too cool.2

### **The Rosenstiel School Heat Burden Study (2023)**

Research from the University of Miami established that Miamians experience temperatures ![][image3] higher on average than the official NWS reports for KMIA.1 This discrepancy is most pronounced in the 0–48 hour window during the transition from the dry to wet season (April–May), where the urban heat island (UHI) effect intensity peaks at approximately ![][image234] (![][image235]).3

## **The National Blend of Models (NBM) v5.0 Framework**

Implemented in April 2026, NBM v5.0 is the foundational guidance for the short-range period.

* **Extension of Hourly Guidance:** Weather elements, including instantaneous ![][image236]\-meter temperature, are now provided in 1-hourly steps through 48 hours (extended from the previous 36-hour limit).  
* **Methodological Shift:** The system has moved from decaying-average logic to **Quantile Mapping (QMD)** for surface temperature. This improves the calibration of probabilistic outputs, particularly for extreme high-end temperature percentiles.  
* **Meta-Ensemble Inputs:** For the 48-hour period, NBM v5.0 integrates higher-resolution ECMWF and HRRR (High-Resolution Rapid Refresh) guidance, weighting them based on the previous 30 days of performance.

## **Hyperlocal Influences and Systematic Biases in Miami**

### **The Sea Breeze Capping Effect**

The most significant determinant of temperature accuracy in the 0–48 hour window is the Atlantic sea breeze front (SBF).

* **Easterly Capping:** Persistent 10–15 mph easterly winds act as a natural air conditioner, preventing KMIA from reaching upper-80s thresholds.5  
* **Frontal Stalling:** If the SBF fails to develop or remains pinned at the coastline, KMIA can experience a ![][image233] to ![][image237] spike above the forecast high due to unchecked solar radiation on the airport tarmac.5

### **Instrument Nuances and Settlement Risks**

Prediction markets like Kalshi settle based on the NWS Daily Climatological Report (CLI).

* **Precision and Rounding:** ASOS stations transmit 1-minute and 5-minute reports with precision to the nearest whole degree Celsius, which can lead to ![][image238] rounding discrepancies when converted to Fahrenheit for the final CLI report.7  
* **Tarmac Spikes:** A 30-minute break in cloud cover over dark airport surfaces can trigger a "YES" resolution for a high-temperature bracket even if the broader city remains cooler.6

## **Implications for Prediction Markets (Kalshi KXHIGHMIA)**

For traders focusing on the KXHIGHMIA series, the 0–48 hour window provides a clear statistical edge:

1. **Market Overconfidence in Extremes:** Markets often overprice low-probability events (e.g., a ![][image239] high in April) while NBM v5.0 percentiles show an 80% chance of staying below ![][image240].  
2. **Expected Calibration Error (ECE):** Recent analysis shows that raw market probabilities for Miami high temperatures have an ECE of 0.01624, which can be improved by 14.8x through isotonic recalibration.  
3. **The "Morning Ramp" Signal:** Observational trends between 8:00 AM and 11:00 AM local time are the strongest predictors of the daily MaxT. If the rate of increase exceeds ![][image241] per hour, the upper temperature brackets (![][image242] over forecast) become significantly more likely.9

## **Best Sources for Hourly Verification**

* **NWS NBM Station Card v5.0:** Detailed hourly guidance for KMIA through 48 hours. [https://vlab.noaa.gov/web/mdl/nbm-textcard-v5.0](https://vlab.noaa.gov/web/mdl/nbm-textcard-v5.0)  
* **KMIA 3-Day History:** Interactive timeseries with 5-minute observational data. [https://www.weather.gov/wrh/timeseries?site=KMIA\&hourly=true](https://www.weather.gov/wrh/timeseries?site=KMIA&hourly=true)  
* **MDL LAMP Verification:** Official statistics for Localized Aviation MOS Program accuracy. [https://vlab.noaa.gov/web/mdl/lamp](https://vlab.noaa.gov/web/mdl/lamp)  
* **Rosenstiel School "Shading Dade" Study:** Analysis of urban heat discrepancy. [https://news.miami.edu/rosenstiel/stories/2023/06/officially-reported-temperatures-underestimate-miamis-heat-burden-new-study-finds.html](https://news.miami.edu/rosenstiel/stories/2023/06/officially-reported-temperatures-underestimate-miamis-heat-burden-new-study-finds.html)

#### **Works cited**

1. Officially reported temperatures underestimate Miami's heat burden, new study finds, accessed May 8, 2026, [https://news.miami.edu/rosenstiel/stories/2023/06/officially-reported-temperatures-underestimate-miamis-heat-burden-new-study-finds.html](https://news.miami.edu/rosenstiel/stories/2023/06/officially-reported-temperatures-underestimate-miamis-heat-burden-new-study-finds.html)  
2. 7A.6 Verification of Gridded Morning Temperatures at NWS Miami, FL Gordon Strassberg\* NOAA/National Weather Service (NWS),, accessed May 8, 2026, [https://ams.confex.com/ams/pdfpapers/160386.pdf](https://ams.confex.com/ams/pdfpapers/160386.pdf)  
3. Study reveals limits of using land surface temperature to explain heat hazards in Miami-Dade County, accessed May 8, 2026, [https://news.miami.edu/rosenstiel/stories/2024/10/study-reveals-limits-of-using-land-surface-temperature-to-explain-heat-hazards-in-miami-dade-county.html](https://news.miami.edu/rosenstiel/stories/2024/10/study-reveals-limits-of-using-land-surface-temperature-to-explain-heat-hazards-in-miami-dade-county.html)  
4. Investigating Urban Heat Islands in Miami, Florida, Utilizing Planet and Landsat Satellite Data \- MDPI, accessed May 8, 2026, [https://www.mdpi.com/2073-4433/16/7/880](https://www.mdpi.com/2073-4433/16/7/880)  
5. Will the high temp in Miami be 86-87° on Apr 17, 2026? \- Tenki, accessed May 8, 2026, [https://trytenki.ai/past-picks/will-the-high-temp-in-miami-be-86-87-on-apr-17-2026](https://trytenki.ai/past-picks/will-the-high-temp-in-miami-be-86-87-on-apr-17-2026)  
6. Will the high temp in Miami be \>86° on Apr 20, 2026? — 87° or above | Tenki, accessed May 8, 2026, [https://trytenki.ai/past-picks/will-the-high-temp-in-miami-be-86-on-apr-20-2026](https://trytenki.ai/past-picks/will-the-high-temp-in-miami-be-86-on-apr-20-2026)  
7. How is Temperature Reported at Airports (like LAX)? \- National Weather Service, accessed May 8, 2026, [https://www.weather.gov/lox/asostemperature](https://www.weather.gov/lox/asostemperature)  
8. metar kmia \- FAA WeatherCams \- Federal Aviation Administration, accessed May 8, 2026, [https://weathercams.faa.gov/map/-80.29,22.37074,6/airport/MIA/details/weather](https://weathercams.faa.gov/map/-80.29,22.37074,6/airport/MIA/details/weather)  
9. Miami, Miami International Airport \- National Weather Service, accessed May 8, 2026, [https://www.weather.gov/data/obhistory/KMIA.html](https://www.weather.gov/data/obhistory/KMIA.html)
